speaker: interviewer
text: Leon Wells interview, tape four. Dr. Wells, you had just told me about the death of your sisters and the feeling among the camp prisoners of the German's victory and what that would mean. Why don't you continue with your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. You get to a point where, let them all know. Let the world know what really it means. And only you don't care about anything anymore. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You get to a point where let the devil win. I'm finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had enough. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: To?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Lemberg. Walked.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In January or February, 1943. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Maybe even earlier. I don't know exactly. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so I found there my two brothers. My father was already taken too, in the mean time. I did not know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you certain of their fate when they had been taken? Was it common knowledge at that point that if you were taken, and you were deported, that you were being murdered?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were-- not common knowledge if it is crematorium or not crematorium, but that they were murdered. Because let us face it, from my viewpoint already, from the beginning, when they took me right the first-- second day on Czwartakow, and these 5,000 people were killed, I knew about it. But as much as I did not touch the bones of these people, I knew that they were shot. It wasn't, at this time, a question that they may have been sent to some resort place. You know, it was common knowledge.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so my two brothers were still-- they were young kids. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and I started, in Lemberg, the life of me and them and working and seeing how to survive. You see, from this time, even before, there was no big problem of food or anything for a very simple reason. Because you lived in a society where every month, a group of people were taken away. You know? And they left a household. So you went into this household, and you got what they left in food, and so on. We had enough food because they always had something prepared. Everybody was afraid of tomorrow. So it wasn't a question of-- you know, it was from a bigger group, a smaller group is not the question. It's a question [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so it was with them. They made the ghetto smaller, meantime. So we moved into an apartment, I with my two brothers. In the Lwow Ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Lwow Ghetto. And another six people in the same small apartment. But somehow, you did not think-- everybody had a bed, two, three people in a bed, so what is the big deal? You know, you did not think in the terms, you know, it's too many or too few people. And so. And somehow you know, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the-- [CHUCKLES] to come to think about, normally you survived with people [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that very often survived because they had-- they were richer. They had-- they were more [NON-ENGLISH]. They had the money. They had the food. They had everything. You know? So we were a part of it. And nobody really thought about it in a different way. And so we worked in this ghetto, in this small ghetto, maybe 10,000, 15,000 people out from 150,000. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- What kind of work did you do in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was still a glazier, and sewers, sewers, I remember. It was a big-- a big job to keep the sewers going. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I had somebody that knew about sewers, so I helped him. I don't even know. I don't really remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by June 1943, they liquidated this ghetto, too, overnight. You know, nobody was even prepared. Nobody even thought about it. Will they? Will not they? Who cares?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took us all, these 10,000 or 15,000 people, to the sands of the Janowska camp again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was again in the same camp with my two brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with-- you were with your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my two brothers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And again here, they had the selection. Younger, and older, and weak, and women, and children, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Describe the process of registering-- you said, 10,000 people--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the selection.

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't-- I don't know, really. And I don't remember exactly. But the selection was once you came in, they took you by truck. And as you walked out, they wrote down all the people. And they all-- first, we were sitting at the-- on the sand, sitting next to each other. All of us sitting, thousands of people, on the sand. It was sandy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and they called out groups. This is group, number one, number two, and this group. Was, you go here, you go here. You go here, you go here. Nobody even knew exactly what it means. And so I was called also with my two brothers, and my two brothers were sent-- they were shot, and I was going to the other side. Probably-- you go here, you go here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No

speaker: interviewer
text: Didn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. You did not know. You did not care. You see, you get to a point where very often, you spoke with people in these-- like, for example, they shot this and these, they shot my brother. Oh, thank God they shot him. He's already over. I have still to suffer. This becomes a daily routine, the daily talking. If a father said, my two children were taken, thank God they are over, you told him. You did not have any feeling. It was-- and you-- and nobody had any feeling of death or life. It isn't that oh, yes, it's a terrible thing. No, they are already over suffering.

speaker: interviewer
text: You spoke about it that you said, how many brothers or sisters did you have? Four or six or two? And where? Oh, they are already over, the tragedy, the suffering. I am the one that still have to suffer. It's like a punishment that you are alive, and they are not. It was you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember very distinctly where they said, oh, they are already over suffering. You did not say they died. They are already over suffering. Finished. And the father would tell you about his two daughters, the two-- they're beautiful and on, but they are already over their suffering. They were already, like, liberated. They are finished. It's you that suffers, not them. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It should be-- a little bit, I want to tell here a story. I don't know you should-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about six months ago, I decided-- not six months, six weeks ago. I decided maybe I should write about the Kevorkian case, what had really happened. And I called up my friend, who is the editor of the The is a Jewish agency magazine. And his first reaction was, you know, according to Jewish law, it's forbidden to commit suicide, you know, according to [NON-ENGLISH]. And I said, fine, so forget about it. And he said, as such, I don't want even to touch it. I said, fine, forget about it. I am going to write it this week. But next week-- and I [INAUDIBLE] him, but please, do me a favor. Read it, and send it right back to me if you don't like it. I said, I am for Kevorkian case, again, of this time. And I wrote it down. Within a few days, he called me up himself at 8 o'clock in the morning, because he thought maybe I will leave the house. He wants it, and so on. And he will publish it very quickly, and so on. And despite that it's against-- and for this reason, that I said, thank God that it's over, the suffering. The suffering is over. The death and-- until you are not there, the idea is so horrible to say about your own children, but they are over. They're already finished with their suffering. Exactly. But. So. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So here I wrote this about Kevorkian. And this is, in this, what I'm trying, because very few people will understand this. And this is what I'm saying. Very few people, if you suffer, can understand this too about Kevorkian. It's easy for us to sit and make in normal conditions, the idea, what it means. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So this is it. So. And I went to the right. And before you knew, they put me in again to the Janowska camp. But they put me in into a group, with a group. Within a few days, they took us all. Probably had to get rid of all these 10,000 people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they brought us over to the-- to the sands, all of us again. We were 40 people in this group. The sand was the gathering place.

speaker: interviewee
text: Near-- no. Near-- near Janowska camp. Why was it they built near Janowska? It was not agriculture, or you could build houses. It was a very sandy neighborhood. And this is what we called the sands. Janowska camp was near the sands. It's why the camp was there, to start. And-- and they took us over maybe half a mile, a mile behind it. Took us over, and we start to build there a tent. In the night, they brought us back to the camp. And once we finished building the tent, they started that we will-- that our job will be in the whole neighborhood, including where they took the people from Czwartakow, and the whole-- for years, I went through it-- to undig all the graves, to clean out all these bodies, and burn them on a fire, and take out the gold from gold teeth, earrings, and so on. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your group-- did this group have a special name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sonderkommando 1005. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was Sonderkommando 1005. But if that's a special name. The Janowska camp had Sonderkommando. Special commander. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, the first day, decided it's finished. It's enough. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like to be assigned to this brigade?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't assigned. Like, in the beginning, they explain to you what the job is. You are not assigned till you first build the thing. And-- and afterwards, to undig the bodies, who cares about the bodies or not the bodies? Because not only you did not care, but as I said before, you looked at the dead like they are over. You have to suffer, not them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And I decided-- but I decided I'm not going to work. Who wants to suffer more? So they beat me, and so on. And I wouldn't do anything. So what they did with me, being young, 17, 17-year-old, 18, 17, 17-and-a-half-year-old. They decided that I will be the orderly, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to keep paper number, because they needed somebody to have order in these two barracks, and also orderly for paper to keep, because they took out the gold teeth and so on to ship to the banks, that I will keep the paper and pencils and so on This what you asked me, why did I take the paper and pencils?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I was the orderly in these things. Again, not working, and so on. The only time that I went out to work, one time they took out-- they did not have enough people-- was to undig the grave where I was to be shot in 1942, when I had the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Typhoid.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Typhus. And there they had-- but they had-- the Germans had to list exactly what they're undigging, how many people are in the grave. And you had to get out to the last body of the last scalp to make sure nothing is left. And we spent the whole day looking, because I was 181. And we had only on the paper 180 bodies, scalps. So where is the 181? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they were looking and looking. Only I, digging there, knew exactly where the 181 is. And after a day, we gave up. We cannot find it, you know. So you have to give up one day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of the purpose for digging up the bodies and burning them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we knew that it is not to leave any signs of atrocities.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many bodies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hundreds of thousands. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the other people in Sonderkommando 1005.

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean by other people? We were about 80 people. Afterwards, 120 people, because we went out of town, and 120 people until November 19, 1943. We made-- we all decided to escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get to that, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] during your time in Janowska, or even before that, was there any observance of the Jewish holidays? I know in one of your books, you talk about Yom Kippur.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In 1943, in the death brigade, it was not an observance of God or of anything. It was more an observance to ourselves that we are still alive. We know what's going on. And it was the day-- the night before. It was the night that we were out on [NON-ENGLISH] Street in Lwow to undig the Polish leadership that did not want to join the German. You see, a lot of people make a basic mistake, in my opinion, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that why in Poland were more people killed than in any other country, even percentage-wise. Not even the numbers, because we were the highest number. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] All other countries, practically, all, and including Denmark, what everybody speaks about Denmark, were collaborators. Joined the Germans and built the government, like the Vichy government in France. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The only country-- the only two or three countries that said no was Poland, Czechoslovakia, and Holland. These are the three countries in Europe. In these three countries, Czechoslovakia, being known for centuries to be the most pro-Semitic country in the world, at this time, had the highest percentage of Jews killed, because the Germans took it over. Theresienstadt was in Czechoslovakia. Heydrich was in Czechoslovakia. Because the-- the countries that collaborated were the best countries for the Jews, including one of the most pro-German was Bulgaria, where 95% of Jews survived, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] including [INAUDIBLE] wife. And this is why Shamir's wife was a leader in Bulgarian Jews. But nobody wants to speak, because they are Eastern. You know, West is different. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is why people escaped from-- from northern France to the [INAUDIBLE] government to the south. This-- in Italy. Italians, they are separate. But also Denmark, because they knew when the action will be. Hungary. Jews were in Poland, let's run away to Hungary. But in Poland, they said, no. We are not going to collaborate. And they were taken and being shot. And we undug their grave to burn them. And we only knew that they were Polish because we found the inscriptions and so on on their pens and paper.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was it that you dug up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Professor Bartel, Ostrowski, Boy-Zelenski, a whole Polish leadership that was. And this is why in Poland, it was so important that I am a witness again and again. And in Poland, they invited me, and so on, many times. But it was Boy-Zelenski, Bartel, and so on. And that was Yom Kippur evening, before Yom Kippur. Yom Kippur evening in 1943. And-- And for us, a lot of us, it was a question of self-respect, like, to keep something. To be-- to-- to be a human being. It wasn't for any religious, and so on, purposes. It was more for us to be a human being. I have something, too. I'm not giving in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the people-- the members of the death brigade, of Sonderkommando 1005, treated differently than the other prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we were treated very well. I mean, I don't mean that we weren't beaten here and there. But we were treated-- we had enough food. Not only enough, more than enough food. We had leftover food. Plus, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] if they brought a group of 1,000 Jews, they told them exactly same as they brought it in '41, '42. You are going to work someplace. So they took with them what they have left in food or in gold to bring with them. They came to us. And all this food or clothing was given to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think they did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean, why?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did they-- why didn't they kill you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they needed a group to undig. They-- who would undig the graves, and so on? They had-- by the way, they had 23 different groups in Germany, in Poland and Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So only three of them or four of them were Russian prisoners for war that did this same job. The only one that made the uprising was our group. Nobody else made the uprising. And they were prisoners of war, soldiers. Tell me about the plans for the uprising and what precipitated [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a plan for escape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Till this time, we did not do anything, because still, Janowska camp was there, maybe. That 5,000 people, 2,000, the last Jews of Lwow who were there. We did not do with anything. Because the whole idea was since-- I tell you, since 1941, since the beginning. If you do something, they will punish the other Jews for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even if you thought about it, and you analyzed, in any case, they will be killed. So who cares? But the idea, who cares, or it's because of you, you don't want to do it. It's today. Nobody thinks what tomorrow. But today, you are doing it. And today they are killed, but tomorrow, who knows what changes [? are ?] [? there? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: After they liquidated the Janowska camp, and when we saw that they are bringing people from Janowska camp, it's finished. The camp is finished. Janowska camp is finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1943, November 1943. Now, we said, we have freedom. For example, our leader, Herches, his mother was still in the Janowska camp. How can you make an uprising when you think that she will be killed because of our want to do something or to show something. You don't-- you know, you have a certain respect. This is different. You know. So once it was liquidated, Janowska camp, we made an uprising, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you said that Herches was the leader.

speaker: interviewee
text: Herches was the head of our group.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. He was one of our--

speaker: interviewer
text: So was there some sort of a-- a government, governing body, within the death brigade?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- in the death brigade, there was no, as such, government. But certain people had certain responsibilities, like I had the responsibility to be the orderly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Record keeping.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did record-- all the orderlies to supply. I did not keep the record. I supplied papers. They brought it to me back. I wasn't there to do the counting. There was another kid doing the counting. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Herches was to be-- to-- to be sure that every group gets up. Every group-- he was the head [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] man in this group. Yet, he could choose where to sleep, where we should all sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. OK. We'll continue in just a moment, but we'll stop here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.
